perturbation.original_text: Integrity agrees to respond to any request for approval within five (5) business days after Integrity's receipt of request and samples.)
perturbation.changed_text: Integrity agrees to respond to any request for approval within one (1) business day after Integrity's receipt of request and samples.)
perturbation.explanation: Changing the response time from five business days to one business day may be unreasonably short, potentially violating standards of good faith and fair dealing, especially if thorough review requires more time.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing requires parties to a contract to act honestly and reasonably in their dealings. A one-business-day response time may be deemed unreasonable, depending on the complexity of the approval required, thus breaching this covenant.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text, by reducing the response time from five business days to one, creates a potential conflict with the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. The original contract allowed a reasonable timeframe for Integrity to respond, while the modified contract may not, depending on the complexity of the request. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines good faith as encompassing 'honest dealing' and 'observance of fair dealing standards'. If one business day is insufficient for a thorough review, Integrity could be seen as acting in bad faith, thus contradicting the law. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight this potential breach of good faith.

perturbation.original_text: Either party, at its sole expense, upon at least thirty (30) days written notice (and not more than once in respect of any accounting period) will have the right to inspect the other party's books regarding the obligations hereunder for a period of two (2) years from the date on which any statement is rendered. Such auditing party must make specific written objection within such two (2) year period. Thereafter, it will be deemed to have consented to any such statements or accountings which will then be considered an account stated as between the parties, not subject to any objection for any reason whatsoever. Provided the objecting party has made timely written objection, as aforesaid, such party may file an action regarding same within two (2) years and six (6) months after the applicable statement is rendered, after which time any such action will be deemed barred.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party, at its sole expense, upon at least ten (10) days written notice (and not more than once in respect of any accounting period) will have the right to inspect the other party's books regarding the obligations hereunder for a period of one (1) year from the date on which any statement is rendered. Such auditing party must make specific written objection within such one (1) year period. Thereafter, it will be deemed to have consented to any such statements or accountings which will then be considered an account stated as between the parties, not subject to any objection for any reason whatsoever. Provided the objecting party has made timely written objection, as aforesaid, such party may file an action regarding same within one (1) year and six (6) months after the applicable statement is rendered, after which time any such action will be deemed barred.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the audit period to one year and the time to file an action to one year and six months may be considered unreasonably short, potentially conflicting with statutes of limitations for breach of contract claims, depending on the jurisdiction.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Statute of Limitations for Breach of Contract
perturbation.law_explanation: Tennessee's statute of limitations for breach of contract is six years. Reducing the period to file an action to one year and six months directly contradicts Tennessee law, making the contractual term potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the audit period and the time to file an action, while the Law Explanation states that Tennessee law has a six-year statute of limitations for breach of contract. Because the contract reduces this time to one year and six months, it directly contradicts Tennessee law. Scraped Law Snippet 2 shows a sign-in page to LexisNexis, a legal database, and indicates access to legal terms and conditions, suggesting resources to verify statute of limitations, and supporting the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of such an Source: INTEGRITY MEDIA INC, 10-K405, 3/29/2001 assignment by either party of its rights to an unrelated third party, the other party shall be given written notice, then in such event the other party may upon six-months (6-months) written notice terminate this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of such an Source: INTEGRITY MEDIA INC, 10-K405, 3/29/2001 assignment by either party of its rights to an unrelated third party, the other party shall be given written notice, then in such event the other party may upon thirty-days (30-days) written notice terminate this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period from six months to thirty days could be considered commercially unreasonable, depending on the industry and the nature of the agreement. This may violate standards for adequate notice in contract law.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-309 requires reasonable notification for contract termination where the contract is indefinite in duration. While this contract has a defined term, similar principles of reasonableness may apply, and a thirty-day notice period may be deemed insufficient to allow the non-assigning party to find a suitable replacement or otherwise protect its interests.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this Article or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the notice period for contract termination from six months to thirty days. The Law Explanation suggests this may be insufficient, potentially violating principles of reasonableness similar to those in UCC § 2-309. Snippet 1 states that termination requires 'reasonable notification', implying a standard that the reduced notice period might not meet, thus creating a contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation supports this by suggesting the change may be commercially unreasonable and violate standards for adequate notice.
